name: Red Sea / Suez Canal
summary: The second critical maritime chokepoint alongside Hormuz. ~12% of global trade transits the Red Sea/Suez corridor. Houthis threatened Red Sea attacks for 28 days; launched first missile at Israel (not shipping) on March 28. If Houthis attack Red Sea shipping, the double-chokepoint scenario activates: Hormuz + Red Sea both disrupted, Cape of Good Hope becomes the only route, shipping rates double again.

signals.content: Houthis entered the war with a missile at Israel on March 28 but have NOT attacked Red Sea shipping (yet). UKMTO and maritime trackers report no new incidents in the Red Sea corridor. However, the Houthi crossing from rhetoric to action materially increases the probability of Red Sea shipping attacks. Michael Knights (Washington Institute) expects incremental escalation: missile at Israel → Red Sea threat → actual shipping attack, spaced 5-10 days apart. Container freight up 3rd consecutive week anticipating disruption. (Sources: UKMTO, IDF, Al Jazeera, Drewry, Maritime Hub)

edges.context: Houthis control Yemen's Red Sea coastline and have demonstrated capability to attack shipping (2024-25 campaign). Currently in the war but haven't attacked shipping yet.
~12% of global trade transits Red Sea/Suez. If Houthis attack shipping, route diversions to Cape of Good Hope add 10-14 days and massive cost.
If BOTH Hormuz and Red Sea are disrupted simultaneously, there is no safe Gulf export route. This is the worst-case scenario for global energy trade.
Saudi Arabia's Yanbu pipeline exports via Red Sea bypass Hormuz. If Red Sea is also disrupted, this bypass becomes useless. Oil to $130+.

trigger_points.condition: Houthis attack commercial shipping in the Red Sea
trigger_points.mechanism: Double chokepoint activated. Cape of Good Hope only route. Shipping rates double. Oil $130+. India acute energy crisis. Container freight spikes. Global trade disrupted.
